title: Dog Excessive Licking of Joints: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Advice
meta_desc: Is your dog licking their joints? Learn about common causes, when to see a vet, and how photos help triage joint discomfort in dogs.
content_html:
TL;DR: Excessive joint licking usually signals underlying pain, allergies, or injury and requires a veterinary evaluation, though it is only an emergency if accompanied by sudden lameness or severe swelling.

Why is my dog licking their joints excessively?

  • The behavior is often an attempt to self-soothe an area that feels sore, itchy, or inflamed.
  • Common underlying causes include osteoarthritis, skin allergies, or minor soft tissue injuries.
  • Boredom can lead to compulsive licking behavior.
  • Untreated licking can cause secondary skin infections or lick granulomas due to constant moisture and friction.

When is joint licking considered a veterinary emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. In most instances, licking the joints is a chronic issue rather than an acute emergency. However, you should seek immediate care if:

  • Your dog is suddenly non-weight bearing.
  • The joint is visibly swollen and hot to the touch.
  • There is a deep, bleeding wound caused by the licking.
  • The condition persists, requiring a scheduled appointment to prevent worsening.

How can taking photos of my dog’s joint help with their diagnosis?

Capturing clear, well-lit photos of the affected area is an invaluable tool for your veterinary team by:

  • Allowing providers to look for subtle signs of inflammation, hair loss, or changes in skin pigmentation.
  • Providing a baseline to track whether a lesion is growing or healing over time.
  • Ensuring your pet receives the most accurate diagnosis and treatment plan through visual monitoring.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Excessive licking of joints in dogs can be associated with several underlying causes. Joint trauma, such as cranial cruciate ligament rupture, can lead to licking due to pain and inflammation. Idiopathic polyarthritis, arthritis of unknown origin, is another potential cause, particularly in large breeds like German Shepherds and retrievers. Septic arthritis, caused by bacterial agents like staphylococci or streptococci, can also result in joint pain and licking. Urgency and triage advice depend on the severity of clinical signs such as lameness, joint swelling, fever, lethargy, and inappetence. Radiographs and joint fluid analysis (cell counts) are essential for diagnosis.

Chapter: Orthopedics, Immunology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1198)

faqs.question: Can joint licking be a sign of arthritis?
faqs.answer: Yes, many dogs lick their joints to provide temporary relief from the dull ache of osteoarthritis or joint inflammation.

faqs.question: How can I stop my dog from licking until the vet visit?
faqs.answer: Using an Elizabethan collar (cone) is the most effective way to prevent self-trauma and allow the skin to heal while waiting for a professional evaluation.

faqs.question: Does licking always mean there is a physical injury?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. While physical pain is a common cause, dogs may also lick their joints due to environmental allergies or psychological factors like anxiety and boredom.
